title: Form Design That Doesn't Hurt
intro: Forms are how users create and edit tasks. Good form design reduces friction; bad form design makes users rage-quit. Judge whether each form practice helps or hurts the experience.

teach.title: Forms Are the Gateway to Your Data
teach.body: Every task in your app starts as a form submission. If the 'Add Task' form is annoying, users will avoid adding tasks — which defeats the entire purpose of a task manager. The goal is to make form interaction feel **effortless**: minimal fields, clear labels, instant feedback.

teach.title: Validation That Helps, Not Punishes
teach.body: Validation should guide users, not scold them. Show errors **inline** next to the field that has the problem, not in a generic alert at the top of the page. Validate on blur (when the user leaves a field) for format checks, and on submit for required fields. Never clear the entire form when validation fails.

teach.title: Forms Are Like a Drive-Through Window
teach.body: The best drive-throughs have a short menu, a clear speaker, and they repeat your order back to confirm. The worst ones have 200 items, a broken speaker, and they make you start over if you change one thing. Your forms should be the fast lane: ask only what's needed, confirm as you go, and never punish the user for making a small mistake.
Every dollar your app earns starts with a user filling out a form. A smooth form experience means more tasks created, more engagement, and more reasons for users to upgrade to your premium plan.

The best form is the shortest form. For task creation, you might only need a title field — make everything else optional or set smart defaults. Users can always edit a task after creating it. Low friction at creation time means more tasks captured.

cards.content: The 'Add Task' form has just one required field (title) with optional fields collapsed behind a 'More options' toggle
cards.explanation: Minimal friction for the most common action. Most tasks just need a title to start. Users who want to add a due date or priority can expand the options, but they're not forced to see them every time.

cards.content: When form validation fails, clearing all fields and showing an alert that says 'Please fix the errors and try again'
cards.explanation: Clearing the form on error is one of the most frustrating UX patterns. The user has to re-enter everything. Keep all field values, highlight the specific problem, and let the user fix just what's wrong.

cards.content: Showing a red border and 'Title is required' message directly below the title field when the user tries to submit an empty form
cards.explanation: Inline validation tells the user exactly what's wrong and where. It's immediately actionable — the user's eyes are already near the field. Much better than a generic error banner at the top.

cards.content: Requiring users to fill in title, description, priority, due date, category, estimated time, and tags before they can create a task
cards.explanation: Seven required fields to add a task is a wall that kills momentum. Most of these should be optional or have sensible defaults. If capturing a task feels like paperwork, users will stop using the app.

cards.content: Allowing users to press Enter to submit the 'Add Task' form so they can quickly add multiple tasks without reaching for the mouse
cards.explanation: Power users live on the keyboard. Enter-to-submit for simple forms (especially the quick-add flow) makes rapid task entry feel natural. It's a small detail that dramatically improves the experience for frequent users.

cards.content: When the user clicks 'Save Task', the button doesn't change and nothing happens for 2 seconds while the data saves, so users click it multiple times
cards.explanation: No loading state creates anxiety and causes duplicate submissions. Disable the button, show a spinner or 'Saving...' text, and re-enable it when done. Users need to know the app heard them.

cards.content: When the 'Add Task' modal opens, automatically focusing the title input so the user can start typing immediately
cards.explanation: Autofocus removes one step (clicking the input) from the most common action. It signals to the user 'go ahead, start typing.' A small detail that makes the form feel responsive and thoughtful.
